1: Devarim (words/matters) opens Deuteronomy: this is Moses's farewell address to all Israel. The book's Hebrew name Devarim (from davar — word, thing, matter) captures the speech-act character of the entire work. The MKT renders as words. Kol-Yisra'el (all Israel) signals the covenant-community address that marks Deuteronomy's rhetoric throughout.
2: Eleven days from Horeb to Kadesh-barnea: the editorial note underscores that what took forty years was an eleven-day journey. The gap between the possible and the actual is the space of unbelief and disobedience. Derekh har Se'ir (by way of Mount Seir) marks the southern route through Edom.
3: The fortieth year, eleventh month, first day: Moses's speech is precisely dated — the final days of the wilderness journey. The chronological precision of Deuteronomy signals its legal-treaty character: covenants are dated.
4: The notice about Sihon and Og grounds the speech in the recent victories YHWH gave Israel — the immediate experiential evidence that YHWH is fulfilling the land-promise. Sihon king of the Amorites and Og king of Bashan are the two paradigmatic defeat-narratives.
5: Ho'il Moshe be'er (Moses began to expound/explain) — ba'ar means to make clear, to dig out, to engrave. The MKT's expound captures the hermeneutical act: Moses is not merely repeating the law but explaining and applying it for the new generation about to enter the land. This is the Deuteronomic interpretive posture.
6: Rav-lakhem shevet ba'har hazeh (you have stayed long enough at this mountain). YHWH initiates the departure from Horeb — the covenant is not just received at Sinai/Horeb but must be carried and lived in the land. The rav-lakhem formula (enough for you) recurs as a divine correction throughout Deuteronomy and Numbers.
7: The land-boundaries enumerated — Lebanon, the Euphrates, the Arabah, the Mediterranean — are the maximal extent of the Abrahamic promise. The MKT preserves the geographic terms (Arabah, Negev, Shephelah) as proper nouns rather than translating each. The seven-people list (Amorites, Canaanites, Hittites, Perizzites, Hivites, Jebusites) reflects the standard conquest inventory.
8: Re'eh natati lifnekhem et-ha'aretz (see, I have given you the land). The perfect tense of divine gift: YHWH speaks of the land as already given, though Israel must still take possession. This theological certainty-before-completion is the pattern of covenant promise. The oath to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob and to their zera (seed/descendants) is the covenant continuity-thread.
9: Moses's self-incapacity speech (lo-uchal levaddi — I cannot carry you alone) echoes Num 11:14 and is the ground for the leadership-structure appointment. The levaddi (alone) mirrors YHWH's aloneness as Creator and sole God (32:12) — Moses confesses the limits of human singular leadership.
10: The stars-of-heaven comparison (kekhokhvei ha-shamayim la-rov) invokes the Abrahamic promise of Gen 15:5 and 22:17. The fulfillment of the multiplication-promise is evidence-in-hand that YHWH keeps his covenants.
11: YHWH Elohei avoteikhem yosef aleikhem (may YHWH add to you a thousand times over). The elef pe'amim (thousand times) is the superlative-multiplication form — the covenant-blessing expected to compound beyond counting. The MKT preserves thousand times.
12: Moses describes his burden as carrying Israel's torach (burden), masa (load), and riv (strife/quarrel). The three terms together describe the full weight of covenant leadership: logistics, adjudication, and conflict management.
13: Anashim chakhamim vnevunim vide'im (wise, understanding, and experienced men): three qualifications for leaders. Chakham (wise) is practical skill; navon (understanding/discerning) is the ability to perceive and evaluate; yado'a (knowing/experienced) is the track record. The MKT renders as wise, discerning, and knowledgeable.
14: The people's assent (tov-ha-davar asher-dibarta la'asot — the thing you have spoken is good to do) is their covenantal participation in the governance structure. Deuteronomy consistently emphasizes the community's responsive role alongside YHWH's commands.
15: The appointment of tribal leaders over thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens: the decimal-hierarchy system. The shotrim (officials/scribes) are distinct from the shoftim (judges) — administrative support for the judicial structure.
16: Shame'u bein acheikhem ushfattem tsedeq (hear between your brothers and judge with righteousness). Tsadeq (righteous/right) is the standard for judgment: not sentiment, not bribery, not fear of faces. The command to hear the small like the great (kaqaton kaggadol) is the legal equality principle.
17: Lo takiru panim ba-mishpat (you shall not show partiality in judgment). Panim (face/presence) in this idiom is the visible markers of status — the judge who responds to the face of the rich or powerful perverts justice. Ki ha-mishpat la-Elohim hu (for judgment belongs to God): the court is always YHWH's court; human judges are his deputies. This grounds judicial independence: they have nothing to fear because the actual judge is divine.
18: The commands given to the judges are the implementation of Mosaic instruction. The phrase va'atsaveh (and I commanded) marks this as executed, not merely intended.
19: The great and terrifying wilderness (ha-midbar ha-gadol v'ha-nora') is the geographic characterization of the Sinai region — the negative space between Horeb and the land. The journey through it is both historical fact and theological test.
20: Moses locates the people at the hill country of the Amorites (har ha-Emori) — the borderland where YHWH's gift becomes Israel's task. Asher YHWH Eloheinu noten lanu (which YHWH our God is giving us) — present participle of ongoing gift.
21: Re'eh natan YHWH Eloheikhah lefaneikha et ha-aretz (see, YHWH your God has given the land before you). Re'eh (see!) is Deuteronomy's characteristic call to perception and faith. Al-tira v'lo tichat (do not fear, do not be dismayed): the double prohibition of fear is the covenant-reassurance formula. Chet (dismay/terror) carries the connotation of the breaking of moral courage.
22: The people's request to send spies (anashim lefaneinu — men before us) originates with them, not YHWH. In Num 13:1-2 YHWH tells Moses to send spies but adds that this is at the people's urging. Deuteronomy's retelling emphasizes the human initiative that led to the catastrophe.
23: Moses's consent to the spy mission is described as good in his own sight (va-yitav be'einai ha-davar). The autobiographical retrospection of Deuteronomy allows Moses to record his own role in the failure.
24: The twelve spies went up to the hill country (ותעלו ההרה): the ascent to survey the land is the moment of decision that will determine the generation's fate.
25: The spies bring back pri ha-aretz (fruit of the land) in their hands — the physical evidence of the land's goodness. Va-yomeru tovah ha-aretz asher YHWH Eloheinu noten lanu (it is a good land that YHWH our God is giving us): the spies' report is accurate about the land's quality. The problem was not the land but the giants.
26: Lo avitem la'alot va-tamru et-pi YHWH Eloheikhem (you were not willing to go up and you rebelled against the mouth/command of YHWH). Marah (rebelled/disobeyed) is the bitterness-rebellion root — the same root as Miriam/Marah (bitter water, Exod 15:23). The rebellion is against the pi (mouth) of YHWH — his spoken command.
27: The murmuring in the tents (va-taragenu be'oholikhem) is the private distrust that the spies' fear amplified. The accusation that YHWH brought them out of Egypt to deliver us into the hand of the Amorites is the covenant-inversion fear: reading YHWH's redemption as deception.
28: The anashim acheinu (our brothers, the scouts) reported am gadol v'ram mimenu (a people greater and taller than us). The Anakim are the giant-descended peoples of Canaan. The walled cities bashot bashamayim (fortified up to heaven) is the hyperbolic fear-language of the defeated spy report.
29: Moses's counter to the fear: lo ta'artsu v'lo tira'u mehen (do not be terrified, do not be afraid of them). The repetition of anti-fear commands throughout the chapter is the theological response to the psychological defeat.
30: YHWH Eloheikhem ha-holeikh lifneikhem hu yilachem lakhem (YHWH your God who goes before you — he will fight for you). The divine-warrior promise: YHWH as the advance guard. Ke-khol asher asah itekhem be-Mitsrayim le-eineikhem (just as he did for you in Egypt before your eyes) — experiential evidence grounds the command to trust.
31: Uva-midbar asher ra'ita asher nasa'akha YHWH Eloheikhah ka'asher yisa-ish et-beno (in the wilderness you saw how YHWH your God carried you as a man carries his son). The father-son metaphor for YHWH's care in the wilderness is the theological heart of this verse — the daily providential carrying that the next generation experienced. This nasa (carry/bear) prefigures the Servant who bears Israel's burdens (Isa 53:4).
32: Uva-davar hazeh einekhem ma'aminim ba-YHWH Eloheikhem (yet in this matter you did not trust YHWH your God). Ma'aminim (trust/believe) is the aman root — the same root as amen. The failure to aman (trust, be firm/stable in) YHWH is the definition of the wilderness generation's sin. The word that means certainty/reliability is also the word for faith.
33: YHWH went before them — in fire by night and cloud by day (ba-esh laila uvhe'anan yomam) — to show them the way (la-tur lahem ha-derekh). The theophanic presence as navigation-guide is Israel's most concrete wilderness experience. The fire-and-cloud is the visible covenant presence.
34: YHWH's anger against Israel's failure to trust: va-yishma YHWH et-qol divreikhem va-yiqtsof (YHWH heard the voice of your words and was angry). Qatsaf (be angry, wrathful) — covenant anger at covenant violation. The judgment that follows is the consequence of the heard-voice of murmuring.
35: The oath-based judgment: not one of these men (anashim ha-ra'im hazeh — this evil generation) will see the good land. The oath sworn (nasati et-yadi — I lifted my hand, i.e., swore) is irrevocable covenant language.
36: The Caleb exception: mille' acharei YHWH (he followed YHWH wholeheartedly/fully). Male (to fill/be full) with acharei (after/behind) is the wholehearted-following idiom. Caleb's faithfulness is the positive counterpart to the generation's faithlessness — the same land the faithless could not enter, the faithful inherit.
37: Gam-bi hitanaf YHWH biglalekhem (YHWH was angry with me also because of you). Moses includes himself in the judgment — biglalekhem (on your account) — a statement of solidarity with the community and also an explanation of his own exclusion from the land (cf. Num 20:7-13).
38: Joshua is appointed Moses's successor (hu yavo'ah sham — he shall enter there). Chazzeq oto (strengthen him/encourage him) — the Mosaic commission to strengthen Joshua. The chazaq (be strong) command will be Joshua's own watchword (Josh 1:6-9).
39: Ve-tapkhem asher amartem labaz yihyeh (your little ones who you said would become plunder). The children who were feared-for in v 22 (tapenu — our children) are those who will enter the land. The generation that protected its children from a supposedly fatal future is excluded, while the children they feared for inherit the promise.
40: The command to turn back to the wilderness (penu lekhem u-se'u ha-midbar) is the reversal-direction: instead of forward into the land, backward into the desert. The Red Sea road (derekh yam-suf) is the way back toward Egypt.
41: The people's belated confession (chatanu la-YHWH — we have sinned against YHWH) and their own initiative to go up and fight is the wrong response to judgment: they confess sin but do not repent — they act on their own strength rather than YHWH's command. Va-takhintu (you girded yourself) is the language of self-equipped military readiness without divine authorization.
42: YHWH's prohibition: lo ta'alu v'lo tilachamu (do not go up and do not fight) — the prohibition comes after the unauthorized decision, not before. The covenant-pattern: YHWH commands, Israel rebels, Israel suffers, Israel tries to self-correct without repentance, YHWH withholds help.
43: Va-temartem et-pi YHWH va-tazidu (you rebelled against YHWH's command and acted presumptuously). Zud/zid (act presumptuously/arrogantly) is the key term for willful sin — acting without authorization, overstepping the covenant boundary. The MKT renders as acted presumptuously.
44: The Amorites defeated them at Seir (ka-asher ta'asnah ha-dvorim — as bees do). The bee-swarm metaphor for the enemy attackers is vivid: numerically overwhelming, painful, unstoppable without YHWH's protective intervention. The defeat at Hormah (va-yakkitu etkhem be-Se'ir ad-Chormah) is the historical confirmation of YHWH's withheld help.
45: Israel's weeping (va-tashvu va-tivku lifnei YHWH) receives no response: YHWH did not hear their voice (v'lo shama YHWH b'qolkhem). The deaf ear of YHWH to willful sinners who cry only after judgment has fallen — not repentance but the pain of consequences — is the sober covenant warning.
46: Va-teshvu b'Qadesh yamim rabbim ka-yamim asher yeshavtem (you stayed at Kadesh many days, all the days that you stayed there). The elliptical ending — the summarizing all the days you stayed there — is the temporal summary of the wilderness waiting before the journey resumes. The staying is the result of the staying-too-long at Horeb (v 6) taken to its ironic extreme: Israel could have been in the land; instead they stayed in the wilderness for thirty-eight more years.
